## Limitations and Future Research

### Order-Book Execution Realism Constraint

A central limitation of the current empirical setup is the use of a
mid-price-like execution proxy for portfolio valuation and reward computation.
This assumption is convenient for controlled RL training, but it is not fully
realistic for live order-book trading.

In production settings, a strategy that demands immediate execution typically
crosses the spread, which introduces a mechanical cost on each trade. Realized
results are also affected by maker/taker fees, queue priority, partial fills,
and latency-dependent adverse selection. These frictions are only partially
represented in the present setup.

The practical implication is that reported backtest performance is likely
optimistic relative to deployable net performance. Strategies with high turnover
are especially sensitive: even small per-trade execution costs can materially
reduce Sharpe ratio and cumulative return. Future research should therefore
evaluate the same agents under explicit bid/ask execution rules, calibrated
fee schedules, and slippage/impact models to quantify the robustness of the
observed edge under realistic microstructure constraints.
